ground_truths: OMURA, Takeshi is listed as one of the inventors of the European Patent Application EP 1 577 413 A1, with an address at JFE STEEL CORPORATION I.P.D., Tokyo 100-0011 (JP).

Fe-Cr-Si based non-oriented electrical steel sheets are used for high-frequency applications such as iron cores of electric car motors, power generators for gas microturbines, and high-frequency reactors. In this context, high-frequency range is defined as a frequency of several hundred hertz or more, particularly about 400 Hz or more, and the steel sheet is noted for having excellent magnetic properties in a high frequency range of 1 kHz or more.

Patent Document 1 assumes a frequency range of less than 1 kHz for use and does not provide sufficient high-frequency magnetic properties in a frequency range of 1 kHz or more, making it unsatisfactory for non-oriented electrical steel sheets required in the range of about 400 Hz to about 50 kHz.

In Patent Documents 2, the steel sheets show superior iron loss at frequencies of 10 kHz or more due to high electrical resistance, but in the high-frequency range of less than 10 kHz, the high electrical resistance from high Si and Cr content does not give satisfactory iron loss, so further improvement is needed.

High-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet is needed for high-speed motors, such as those used in electric vehicles, because these motors are designed for high-speed rotation to achieve high torque, and the rotor structure includes very narrow bridge portions (1 to 2 mm wide) between magnets and the steel sheet. This narrow width requires the steel sheet to have high strength to prevent failure during operation. Other high-strength materials used before, like non-oriented electrical steel sheets strengthened by adding Mn and Ni, could not provide enough strength and also reduced toughness, leading to poor productivity and yield, and the cost was high due to expensive alloying elements like Ni. Methods using carbonitride dispersion or Cu precipitates also failed to achieve sufficient strength or caused problems like coarsening of crystal grains, reduced fracture elongation, or magnetic aging due to carbide precipitation at motor operating temperatures. Therefore, a high-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet with a specific composition and controlled microstructure is required to meet the demands of high-speed motors in electric vehicles.

High-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet get good high-frequency magnetic properties by controlling the number of nitrides containing chromium inside the steel sheet. If too many nitrides with chromium form during annealing, iron loss gets worse, especially at high frequency. By adjusting the annealing atmosphere, like using less nitrogen gas or adding elements like Al, Sn, or Sb, the amount of nitrides with chromium can be kept low (2,500 or less per mm2), so the steel shows better high-frequency magnetic properties and lower iron loss.

The addition of bismuth (Bi) to a non-oriented electrical steel sheet plays a critical role in suppressing the formation of Ti inclusions, such as TiN, TiS, and TiC, which can otherwise degrade the magnetic properties of the steel. According to the context, when an appropriate amount of Bi is present (typically between 0.001 mass% and 0.01 mass%), the production of Ti inclusions after annealing is reduced, leading to enhanced crystal grain growth and improved magnetic properties (see <9-hop>). This effect is particularly important for high-speed motor rotors, where low core loss and high efficiency are required (<2-hop>, <3-hop>, <4-hop>).

To maximize the beneficial effects of Bi addition, several compositional and process conditions must be met:
- The steel composition should include Si (1.0–3.5 mass%), Al (0.1–3.0 mass%), Mn (0.1–2.0 mass%), Ti (0.001–0.01 mass%), and Bi (0.001–0.01 mass%), with low levels of C (≤0.01 mass%), P (≤0.1 mass%), S (≤0.005 mass%), and N (≤0.005 mass%) (<8-hop>, <11-hop>).
- The Bi must be added to the molten steel shortly before solidification, ideally within three minutes, to ensure sufficient Bi remains in solution and is not lost due to volatilization (<11-hop>).
- The presence of sulfur (S) must be controlled. If S exceeds 0.005 mass%, desulfurizing elements such as rare earth metals (REM) or Ca should be added to reduce free S, preventing Bi from being trapped in MnS and ensuring it remains available to suppress Ti inclusions (<10-hop>).

When these conditions are satisfied, Bi effectively suppresses the precipitation of TiN and TiS during annealing and TiC during stress relief annealing, resulting in fewer inclusions, larger grain size, and improved magnetic properties, such as lower core loss (W10/800 or W10/1000) (<9-hop>, <11-hop>). This directly supports the requirements for high-speed motor rotors, where both high tensile strength and low high-frequency core loss are essential for efficiency and miniaturization (<2-hop>, <3-hop>, <4-hop>).

Forming an Fe-Ni alloy film on a high-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet significantly improves the relative permeability and reduces the core loss of the material. The optimal improvement is achieved when the Fe-Ni alloy film contains 60% to 90% Ni and has a thickness of at least 0.1 μm, with the effect saturating above 0.6 μm. For the steel sheet itself, key compositional considerations include maintaining C content at 0.05% or less to avoid increased core loss, Si content between 0.1% and 7% for sufficient resistivity without compromising cold-rolling performance, and Al content between 0.01% and 7% for electrical resistance and deoxidation. Additionally, Mn, Ti, N, S, Sn, Cu, and Ni should be controlled within specified ranges to optimize magnetic properties and inhibit surface nitridation and oxidation during annealing.

Steel slabs in Table 3 with Sn (condition 13) or Cu (condition 14) have better magnetic properties than those without Sn and Cu (condition 11). Also, rolling reduction in the second cold-rolling affects results: when it is less than 40% (condition 12) or over 85% (condition 15), magnetic properties are worse than when it is around 68% (conditions 11, 13, 14).

For Examples 1-8, as the Si-Mg phase volume percentage decreases from 4.54 (Example 1) to 1.35 (Example 8), the Cr content ratio increases from 0.5 to 49.2. This indicates an inverse relationship: lower Si-Mg phase volume percentages correspond to higher Cr content ratios in the alloy layer. In contrast, Comp. Ex. 1 shows a much lower Si-Mg phase volume percentage (0.18) and a significantly higher Cr content ratio (76.3), further supporting this trend. Therefore, Table 5 demonstrates that as the Si-Mg phase volume percentage decreases, the Cr content ratio in the alloy layer tends to increase across the examples provided.

Table 1 lists the components of the steel slab as C (0.0019%), Si (2.91%), Al (0.48%), Mn (0.27%), and P (0.022%), with the balance being Fe and inevitable impurities. These components were used to produce steel slabs that underwent hot-rolling and two stages of cold-rolling, with specific processing conditions detailed in the experiments. After processing, magnetic properties such as magnetic flux density B50 and iron loss W15/50 were measured. The results, as shown in Table 2, indicate that by using the components from Table 1 and optimizing the rolling and annealing conditions, the steel achieved improved magnetic properties in the rolling direction, demonstrating the importance of both composition and processing in achieving desirable electrical steel characteristics.

The addition of REMs (rare earth metals) to steel leads to the formation of REM oxysulfides, which have a crystalline structure closely resembling that of TiN. This similarity promotes the coprecipitation of TiN on REM oxysulfides when the amounts of REM, O, S, Ti, and N are within suitable ranges. TiN precipitates at high temperatures (1200 to 1300°C) during processes like casting or slab reheating and becomes fixed in the steel. Once Ti is fixed as TiN, it does not redissolve during lower temperature processes such as finish annealing or stress relief annealing, preventing the precipitation of TiC, which typically forms at lower temperatures (700 to 800°C). As a result, the formation of fine TiC is suppressed, leading to low core loss non-oriented electrical steel sheet with good growth of crystal grains.

[0001] The present invention relates to Fe-Cr-Si based non-oriented electrical steel sheets for high-frequency use which are suitable for iron cores of electric car motors, power generators for gas microturbines, high-frequency reactors, etc. Herein, the term "high-frequency range" is defined as a frequency range of several hundred hertz or more, and in particular, of about 400 Hz or more. The present invention more particularly relates to a steel sheet having excellent magnetic properties in a high frequency range of 1 kHz or more.

[0002] Recently, devices used in the higher frequency ranges than conventionally used, for example, electric car motors, gas microturbines, and high-frequency reactors, have been increasingly used, and there has been a demand for electrical steel sheets having excellent magnetic properties in the high frequency range. These devices are used in a high frequency range of several hundred hertz to several tens of kilohertz.

[0004] However, in the conventional Fe-Si based non-oriented electrical steel sheet for high-frequency use, the Si content in the steel is 3.5% by mass or less, and the electrical resistance of the steel is not so high. In particular, in a high frequency range of 1 kHz or more, the iron loss is high, which is disadvantageous. Therefore, in order to meet the recent needs in society, development of new electrical steel sheets suitable for use in the high frequency range is absolutely necessary.

[0006] A technique for increasing the electrical resistance of steel by adding Cr, Al, etc., to steel without increasing the Si content is disclosed in Patent Document 1. However, in the technique disclosed in Patent Document 1, a frequency range of less than 1 kHz is assumed for use as in the conventional electrical steel sheet for high-frequency use, and it is not possible to obtain sufficient high-frequency magnetic properties in a frequency range of 1 kHz or more. Thus, the steel sheet disclosed in Patent Document 1 does not have a satisfactory effect as a non-oriented electrical steel sheet for high-frequency use suitable in the range of about 400 Hz to about 50 kHz required in recent years. Additionally, the Si content in Patent Document 1 does not exceed that of a typical silicon steel sheet, and rather, Patent Document 1 mainly targets a low-silicon steel sheet with a Si content of about 1.5%.

[0009] Accordingly, it is an object of the present invention to advantageously solve the problem that the high electrical resistance obtained by the high Si content and the high Cr content is not sufficiently reflected to iron loss in a highfrequency range of less than 10 kHz and to provide an Fe-Cr-Si based non-oriented electrical steel sheet having excellent magnetic properties in the high-frequency range, in particular, in a frequency range of 1 kHz or more.

[0010] As a result of intensive research into the problems described above, the present inventors have found that, although the percentage of eddy current loss in the iron loss is generally high in the high-frequency range, the influence of hysteresis loss is relatively large with respect to the Fe-Cr-Si based electrical steel sheet. It has been found that because of a deterioration of hysteresis loss, the decrease in eddy current loss due to high electrical resistance does not sufficiently contribute to high-frequency magnetic properties. It has been discovered that in order to obtain improved hysteresis loss, it is necessary to control the frequency of nitrides containing chromium (nitrides including chromium) in the interior of the steel sheet. The present invention has been achieved based on the findings described above.

Si is a principal element for increasing the electrical resistance of steel. Furthermore, due to the synergic effect with Cr, Si significantly increases electrical resistance, and in particular, Si is an effective element for improving iron loss in the high frequency range. If the Si content is less than about 2.5% by mass, even if Cr is used together, only an electrical resistance that is substantially the same as the electrical resistance of the conventional electrical steel sheet is obtained, and thus it is not possible to obtain satisfactory iron loss in the high frequency range. On the other hand, if the Si content exceeds about 10% by mass, even if Cr is added, toughness that normally allows the steel to be rolled is not ensured. Therefore, the Si content is set at about 2.5% to about 10% by mass. The upper limit may be set at 10.0% by mass.

[0002] In recent years, in terms of prevention of global warming and the like, a further reduction in power consumption in a motor of an air conditioner, a main motor of an electric vehicle, and the like has been required. These motors are often used by being rotated at high speed. Accordingly, a non-oriented electrical steel sheet used for an iron core of a motor has been required to improve (reduce) a core loss in a frequency region of 400 Hz to 800 Hz higher than 50 Hz to 60 Hz being a commercial frequency. This is because the reduction in core loss reduces power consumption, thereby allowing an amount of energy consumption to be reduced.

[0004] On the other hand, since rotating equipment such as motors or generators makes use of electromagnetic phenomenon, the core materials of iron cores of rotating equipment are also required to have excellent magnetic properties. In particular, it is necessary for rotors of high speed motors to assume low iron loss at high frequency; iron loss at high frequency would otherwise lead to a rise in core temperature due to the eddy current induced by a high-frequency magnetic flux, causing thermal demagnetization of embedded permanent magnets, reducing motor efficiency, and so on. Therefore, there is a demand for such an electrical steel sheet as a material for rotors that possesses high strength and excellent magnetic properties.

[0001] The present invention relates to a non-oriented electrical steel sheet, and in particular, to a non-oriented electrical steel sheet having high strength and excellent fatigue properties, and furthermore, excellent magnetic properties that is suitably used for components that are subject to high stress, typically, drive motors for turbine generators, electric vehicles and hybrid vehicles, or rotors for high-speed rotating machinery, such as servo motors for robots, machine tools or the like, and a method for manufacturing the same. [0012] As described above, some proposals have been made on high-strength non-oriented electrical steel sheets. In the proposals made to date, however, it has not been possible until now to manufacture, with the use of an ordinary facility for manufacturing electrical steel sheets, such a high-strength non-oriented electrical steel sheet in an industrially stable manner with good yield and at low cost that has good magnetic properties in addition to high tensile strength and high fatigue strength, and furthermore, satisfy the quality requirements of steel sheet, such as those relating to surface defects, internal defects, sheet shape or the like. Particularly, the high-strength electrical steel sheets that have so far been provided for rotors of high speed motors are in a situation where the resulting rotors will be subject to unavoidable heat generation due to their magnetic property, i.e., high iron loss at high frequency, which necessarily poses limitations on the design specification of the motors.

[0022] According to the present invention, a non-oriented electrical steel sheet may be provided that is excellent in both mechanical properties and magnetic properties required for a rotor material of motors rotating at high speed, and that has excellent quality of steel sheet in terms of scab, sheet shape, and so on. The present invention also allows stable production of such non-oriented electrical steel sheets with high yield, without incurring a significant increase in cost or imposing severe constraints on manufacture or requiring extra steps, as compared to the normal production of non-oriented electrical steel sheets. Therefore, the present invention is applicable in the field of motors, such as drive motors of electric vehicles and hybrid vehicles or servo motors of robots and machine tools, where demand for higher rotational speed is expected to grow in the future. Thus, the present invention has a high industrial value and makes a significant contribution to the industry.

[0024] The reason why the relative permeability Ps is extremely low when the thickness of the Fe-Ni alloy film is less than 0.1 Pm is that magnetic domains on a surface of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet are not easily aligned despite forming the Fe-Ni alloy film. Further, the reason why the effect of improving the relative permeability Ps is saturated is that as long as the magnetic domains on the surface of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet are sufficiently aligned, even though the Fe-Ni alloy film having the thickness of the above or more is formed, the magnetic domains are not easily aligned any more.

[0026] This is because when excitation, a magnetic flux concentrates in a region to be most easily magnetized in a thickness direction of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet. That is, the reason is that although the Fe-Ni alloy film is formed on a single region, (one surface) in the thickness direction, or the Fe-Ni alloy films are formed on two regions (both surfaces) in the thickness direction, it is in a single region that the magnetic flux concentrates most.

The ratio of the content ratio of Cr in the alloy layer to the content ratio of Cr in the plating layer is more preferably 3 to 40 and even more preferably 4 to 25. [0002] More specifically, the present invention makes TiN sufficiently coprecipitate in REM sulfides in non-oriented electrical steel sheet and thereby provides non-oriented electrical steel sheet which decreases the solid solution Ti in the steel, suppresses the precipitation of fine TiC easily occurring at low temperature parts when annealing the steel sheet, and as a result is superior in crystal grain growth and low in core loss.

That is, the inventors of the present invention investigated in detail how Ti, which is a major carbonitride forming element, affects the quality of steel sheet in terms of strengthening by precipitation, recrystallization, grain growth behavior, scabs, and so on. answers: OMURA, Takeshi is identified as an inventor associated with the European Patent Application EP 1 577 413 A1, according to [Source 9]. This information is based on the patent application's preamble, which typically lists the inventors and their affiliations. However, further details about OMURA's role or contributions in this specific patent are not provided in the excerpts.

The Fe-Cr-Si steel addressed in the patent [Source 1] is designed for high-frequency use, particularly suitable for iron cores of electric car motors, power generators for gas microturbines, high-frequency reactors, etc. The high-frequency range here refers to a frequency range of several hundred hertz or more, and in particular, of about 400 Hz or more [Source 1]. However, it's important to note that the patent also mentions a specific focus on a high-frequency range of 1 kHz or more [Source 1, Source 5].

The steel is designed to have excellent magnetic properties in this high-frequency range. According to [Source 7], Si plays a principal role in increasing the electrical resistance of the steel, which significantly improves iron loss in the high-frequency range. The optimal Si content is set between about 2.5% and 10% by mass [Source 7]. Additionally, the presence of chromium (Cr) contributes to this improvement due to a synergistic effect with Si [Source 7].

The patent also highlights that conventional Fe-Si based non-oriented electrical steel sheets for high-frequency use have several limitations. For instance, they have low electrical resistance and high iron loss in the high-frequency range of 1 kHz or more [Source 3]. The invention aims to address these issues by providing an Fe-Cr-Si based non-oriented electrical steel sheet with improved magnetic properties in the high-frequency range [Source 5].

The patent also discusses the importance of controlling the frequency of nitrides containing chromium (nitrides including chromium) in the interior of the steel sheet to obtain improved hysteresis loss, which is crucial for high-frequency magnetic properties [Source 6]. Lastly, it's worth noting that there are other patents focusing on reducing core loss in high-speed motors and improving magnetic properties at higher frequencies [Sources 8, 9], further emphasizing the importance of this area of research.

However, it should be noted that the patent excerpts do not provide specific details about how the steel's composition and microstructure are optimized to achieve these improvements in high-frequency magnetic properties.

The main limitation of Patent Document 1 regarding high-frequency magnetic properties in electrical steel sheets is that it does not provide sufficient high-frequency magnetic properties in a frequency range of 1 kHz or more [Source 6]. Additionally, the Si content in Patent Document 1 does not exceed that of a typical silicon steel sheet, and it primarily targets a low-silicon steel sheet with a Si content of about 1.5% [Source 6]. This limitation is further highlighted by the fact that the technique disclosed in Patent Document 1 assumes a frequency range of less than 1 kHz for use as in conventional electrical steel sheets for high-frequency use [Source 6].

According to [Source 4], the problem with the steel sheet described in Patent Document 2 is that it does not provide sufficient strength, despite the disclosure of non-oriented electrical steel sheets in which carbonitrides are dispersed in steel to achieve strengthening. Additionally, as mentioned in [Source 6], these steel sheets exhibit superior iron loss in response to high electrical resistance in a frequency range of 10 kHz or more. However, it is not explicitly stated whether this lack of strength and the specific issue with high-frequency use are related or separate problems for the steel sheet described in Patent Document 2. [Source 4] does not provide detailed information about the high-frequency magnetic properties of the steel sheets disclosed in Patent Document 2, which might be necessary to fully understand the problem they face in this frequency range.

High-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheets are essential for high-speed motors in electric vehicles due to several reasons and associated problems with other high-strength materials used previously.

Firstly, as stated in [Source 6] and [Source 8], there is a growing demand for small-sized high-power motors for electric vehicles that can rotate at high speeds to generate high torque. The high-speed rotation necessitates the use of a high-strength steel sheet to withstand the stresses generated during operation.

Secondly, according to [Source 7] and [Source 9], high-speed rotation motors for electric vehicles have a narrow bridge portion in their outer periphery, which requires a high-strength steel sheet to ensure structural integrity. In contrast, conventional non-oriented electrical steel sheets may not meet the strength requirements for these applications.

Lastly, as mentioned in [Source 2], high-speed motors are often subjected to unavoidable heat generation due to their magnetic properties, leading to limitations on motor design specifications. High-strength non-oriented electrical steel sheets with excellent magnetic properties, such as those described in [Source 3] and [Source 4], can help mitigate these issues by reducing iron loss at high frequency, thereby preventing thermal demagnetization of embedded permanent magnets and improving motor efficiency.

However, it is worth noting that, according to [Source 2], the industrial production of high-strength non-oriented electrical steel sheets with good magnetic properties, low cost, and minimal quality compromises has been a challenge until now. The patent excerpts do not provide specific details on the problems encountered with other high-strength materials used before in electric vehicle motors.

The high-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheets achieve good high-frequency magnetic properties through a specific composition and control of nitrides containing chromium, as detailed in [Source 1], [Source 4], [Source 8], and [Source 10]. According to these sources, the steel contains 2.5% to 10% by mass of Si, 1.5% to 20% by mass of Cr, and various other elements such as Al, Sb, Sn, Ti, Nb, C, N, S, and incidental impurities. The electrical resistivity of the steel is required to be 60 µΩcm or more [Source 1, Source 4].

The number of nitrides containing chromium per mm² in the interior of the steel sheet is controlled to be 2,500 or less [Source 1, Source 4]. This control is crucial because it helps improve hysteresis loss, which is significant in the high-frequency range for Fe-Cr-Si based electrical steel sheets [Source 2]. The decrease in eddy current loss due to high electrical resistance does not sufficiently contribute to high-frequency magnetic properties when hysteresis loss deteriorates [Source 2]. By controlling nitrides containing chromium, the present invention achieves improved hysteresis loss and provides non-oriented electrical steel sheets with excellent magnetic properties in the high-frequency range [Source 1, Source 3].

However, it is not explicitly stated in the provided excerpts how the control of nitrides containing chromium improves hysteresis loss or what specific role these nitrides play in the overall magnetic properties of the steel sheets. Further investigation may be necessary to gain a more comprehensive understanding of this aspect.

The addition of bismuth (Bi) to a non-oriented electrical steel sheet significantly influences the suppression of Ti inclusions and the resulting magnetic properties. According to [Source 2], [Source 3], and [Source 6], as the Bi content increases, Ti inclusions are reduced, and crystal grains are likely to grow, thereby improving the magnetic property. However, when the Bi content exceeds 0.01 mass%, the magnetic property becomes poor due to a large number of Bi inclusions ([Source 2]).

The optimal range for Bi content in the steel sheet is not explicitly stated in the provided excerpts, but it is suggested that an appropriate amount of Bi should be contained in the non-oriented electrical steel sheet to reduce Ti inclusions and improve magnetic properties ([Source 3], [Source 6]). The exact amount might depend on the Ti content, as shown in [Source 6] where a proportional relationship between the Ti content and the Bi content is established.

For high-speed motor rotor applications, a high-strength steel sheet is required due to the large stress acting on the rotor ([Source 5]). The patent excerpts suggest that the addition of bismuth can help meet this requirement by reducing Ti inclusions and improving the magnetic properties of the steel sheet ([Source 3], [Source 6]).

It's worth noting that the mechanism in which the production of Ti inclusions is suppressed when Bi is contained in the non-oriented electrical steel sheet has not been fully clarified yet ([Source 6]), and more studies might be needed to optimize the compositional and process conditions for maximizing these effects.

The patent excerpts do not provide specific technical details or formulas regarding the manufacturing process of the steel sheets with added bismuth. Further investigation is required to understand the exact process conditions necessary to achieve the desired properties for high-speed motor rotor applications.

Forming an Fe-Ni alloy film on a high-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet significantly improves its magnetic properties, particularly the relative permeability and core loss [Source 1]. The key compositional considerations for the Fe-Ni alloy film are a Ni content of 60% to 90% and a thickness of 0.1 Pm or more [Source 1]. For the steel sheet, it is important that it contains specific amounts of C, Si, and Al, with typical values being C: 0.003%, Si: 2.4%, and Al: 0.5% [Source 2]. The thickness of the steel sheet can vary, but a common value is 0.35 mm [Source 2, Source 4].

The Fe-Ni alloy films can be formed using an electroplating or immersion plating method [Source 1, Source 4, Source 6]. The thickness of the Fe-Ni alloy film can range from 0.05 Pm to 0.8 Pm, with optimal values for improving magnetic properties being around 0.5 Pm per one surface [Source 4]. The Ni content of the Fe-Ni alloy films can vary from 0% to 100%, but optimal values are between 78% and 60% when the thickness is constant at 0.3 Pm [Source 5].

The reason for these specific compositional requirements is that they allow for the alignment of magnetic domains on the surface of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet, which improves its magnetic properties [Source 9]. However, it's important to note that when the thickness of the Fe-Ni alloy film is less than 0.1 Pm, the relative permeability remains extremely low due to the difficulty in aligning magnetic domains [Source 9]. Additionally, once the magnetic domains are sufficiently aligned, further increases in the thickness of the Fe-Ni alloy film do not significantly improve the magnetic properties any further [Source 9].

It's also worth mentioning that the Fe-Ni alloy films can contain other metallic elements such as Mo to further enhance their magnetic properties [Source 8]. However, the patent does not provide specific details on the effects of these additional elements.

In summary, forming an Fe-Ni alloy film on a high-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet improves its magnetic properties by allowing for the alignment of magnetic domains. The key compositional considerations are a Ni content of 60% to 90% and a thickness of 0.1 Pm or more for the Fe-Ni alloy film, and specific amounts of C, Si, and Al for the steel sheet. Further research may be needed to understand the effects of additional metallic elements in the Fe-Ni alloy films. [Source 1, Source 2, Source 4, Source 5, Source 8, Source 9]

The steel slab components that give better magnetic properties in the rolling direction, according to [Source 4], are those containing Sn or Cu, as seen from the results presented in Table 4. Specifically, the condition No. 13, which contains Sn, and the condition No. 14, which contains Cu, showed improved magnetic properties compared to the condition No. 11, where neither Sn nor Cu was present.

Regarding the rolling reduction's effect on results, [Source 2] states that in the condition where hot-rolled sheet annealing is not performed, the magnetic properties in the rolling direction of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet can be significantly improved by appropriately combining the finish temperature in the hot-rolling and the rolling reduction in the second cold-rolling. More specifically, when the finish temperature in the hot-rolling is 900°C or less, and the rolling reduction in the second cold-rolling is not less than 40% nor more than 85%, it is possible to obtain extremely good magnetic properties in the rolling direction ([Source 2]).

Furthermore, [Source 1] provides specific examples of rolling reductions in the first and second cold-rolling processes. In these examples, a rolling reduction in the first cold-rolling was set to approximately 40% ([Source 1], Table 4), and a rolling reduction in the first cold-rolling ranged from 31.4% to 36.4% ([Source 5], Table 2). These examples demonstrate that the rolling reduction in the first cold-rolling can indeed vary within the specified range while still achieving improved magnetic properties in the rolling direction.

However, it's important to note that the patent excerpts do not provide a comprehensive list of all steel slab components and their effects on magnetic properties or a detailed explanation of how the rolling reduction affects these properties beyond the given examples and general guidelines. For a more complete understanding, further investigation of the entire patent document may be necessary.

The relationship between the Si-Mg phase volume percentage and the Cr content ratio in the alloy layer can be observed by comparing the Surface layer Cr content (in % by weight) and the Si-Mg phase area ratio on the plating layer surface for different examples in Table 5.

This suggests that an increase in Cr content in the alloy layer promotes the growth of the Si-Mg phase in the plating layer, as growth of the Si-Mg phase is promoted by Cr according to [Source 7].

When comparing Examples 1-8 with Comp. Ex. 1, it can be seen that both the Surface layer Cr content and the Si-Mg phase area ratio on the plating layer surface increase as the examples progress from 1 to 8. However, Comp. Ex. 1 has a significantly higher Surface layer Cr content (5.10% by weight) compared to Examples 1-8 (ranging from 0.03% to 2.50% by weight). Correspondingly, the Si-Mg phase area ratio on the plating layer surface for Comp. Ex. 1 is much higher than that of Examples 1-8. This indicates that a higher Cr content in the alloy layer results in a greater increase in the Si-Mg phase volume percentage in the plating layer.

It's important to note that [Source 10] states that the plating layer contains 0.2% to 15% by volume of an Si-Mg phase, but the examples provided in Table 5 have a much lower volume percentage of the Si-Mg phase (ranging from 3.0% to 32.0%). This suggests that the examples may not fully comply with the range specified in [Source 10].

The components listed in Table 1 for the steel slab are C: 0.017 mass%, Si: 3.12 mass%, Al: 0.65 mass%, Ni: 2.54 mass%, P: 0.02 mass%, N: 0.003 mass%, Ti: 0.18 mass%, and Fe (implicit, as a balance component).

According to [Source 1], the magnetic properties measured after processing include core loss W15/50, magnetic flux density B50, and core loss W10/1000 for the non-oriented electrical steel sheet. In [Source 2] and [Source 3], it is mentioned that these magnetic properties were measured from Epstein samples cut out from the non-oriented electrical steel sheets after processing.

However, it's important to note that the relationship between these components and the magnetic properties isn't explicitly stated in the provided excerpts. To fully understand this relationship, one would need additional information about how the specific concentrations of each component affect the magnetic behavior of the steel alloy.

[Source 4] suggests that Ti and Nb deteriorate magnetic properties, while [Source 5] indicates that B content can have an impact on magnetic flux density. Therefore, it's reasonable to assume that the concentrations of these elements could influence the measured magnetic properties. However, more detailed information is required for a definitive conclusion.

The addition of REMs (Rare Earth Metals) to steel significantly influences the precipitation of TiN and TiC, as detailed in several patents.

According to [Source 1] and [Source 2], the REMs facilitate the coprecipitation of TiN in REM sulfides within non-oriented electrical steel sheet. This coprecipitation results in a decrease in solid solution Ti in the steel, which in turn suppresses the precipitation of fine TiC at low temperature parts during annealing. The suppression of TiC precipitation leads to improved crystal grain growth and lower core loss in the resulting steel.

However, it's important to note that other REM inclusions may form differently than REM oxysulfides [Source 8]. When suitable conditions are set to enable TiN coprecipitation with these specific REM oxysulfides, a larger amount of Ti can be fixed as TiN on the REM oxysulfides, further strengthening the suppression of TiC precipitation [Source 4].

In contrast, Ca and Mg, which are desulfurization elements, have little effect on causing coprecipitation of TiN compared to REMs [Source 5].

Bi, another element mentioned in a separate patent [Source 6], is found to suppress the precipitations of TiN and TiS during annealing processes. This suppression also extends to the prevention of TiC precipitation during stress relief annealing [Source 6].

It's worth mentioning that the inventors of one patent [Source 3] investigated the effects of Ti on steel quality, finding that when added so that the resulting Ti content is equal to or less than the total content of C and N in atomic fraction, Ti has an optimum range of addition for satisfying high-level requirements regarding both high strength and magnetic properties.

In summary, the addition of REMs to steel influences the precipitation of TiN and TiC by facilitating the coprecipitation of TiN on specific REM oxysulfides, leading to improved crystal grain growth and lower core loss in electrical steel. Other elements like Bi may also play a role in suppressing the precipitation of Ti-containing compounds during annealing processes. However, more information is needed to fully understand the optimum range of addition for Ti when considering other factors such as high strength and magnetic properties.

detailed_results.question: What Fe-Cr-Si steel do for high frequency, like in electric car motors and reactors, and what frequency is high frequency mean here?
detailed_results.ground_truth: Fe-Cr-Si based non-oriented electrical steel sheets are used for high-frequency applications such as iron cores of electric car motors, power generators for gas microturbines, and high-frequency reactors. In this context, high-frequency range is defined as a frequency of several hundred hertz or more, particularly about 400 Hz or more, and the steel sheet is noted for having excellent magnetic properties in a high frequency range of 1 kHz or more.
contexts: [0001] The present invention relates to Fe-Cr-Si based non-oriented electrical steel sheets for high-frequency use which are suitable for iron cores of electric car motors, power generators for gas microturbines, high-frequency reactors, etc. Herein, the term "high-frequency range" is defined as a frequency range of several hundred hertz or more, and in particular, of about 400 Hz or more. The present invention more particularly relates to a steel sheet having excellent magnetic properties in a high frequency range of 1 kHz or more.

[0002] Recently, devices used in the higher frequency ranges than conventionally used, for example, electric car motors, gas microturbines, and high-frequency reactors, have been increasingly used, and there has been a demand for electrical steel sheets having excellent magnetic properties in the high frequency range. These devices are used in a high frequency range of several hundred hertz to several tens of kilohertz.

[0004] However, in the conventional Fe-Si based non-oriented electrical steel sheet for high-frequency use, the Si content in the steel is 3.5% by mass or less, and the electrical resistance of the steel is not so high. In particular, in a high frequency range of 1 kHz or more, the iron loss is high, which is disadvantageous. Therefore, in order to meet the recent needs in society, development of new electrical steel sheets suitable for use in the high frequency range is absolutely necessary.

[0006] A technique for increasing the electrical resistance of steel by adding Cr, Al, etc., to steel without increasing the Si content is disclosed in Patent Document 1. However, in the technique disclosed in Patent Document 1, a frequency range of less than 1 kHz is assumed for use as in the conventional electrical steel sheet for high-frequency use, and it is not possible to obtain sufficient high-frequency magnetic properties in a frequency range of 1 kHz or more. Thus, the steel sheet disclosed in Patent Document 1 does not have a satisfactory effect as a non-oriented electrical steel sheet for high-frequency use suitable in the range of about 400 Hz to about 50 kHz required in recent years. Additionally, the Si content in Patent Document 1 does not exceed that of a typical silicon steel sheet, and rather, Patent Document 1 mainly targets a low-silicon steel sheet with a Si content of about 1.5%.

[0009] Accordingly, it is an object of the present invention to advantageously solve the problem that the high electrical resistance obtained by the high Si content and the high Cr content is not sufficiently reflected to iron loss in a highfrequency range of less than 10 kHz and to provide an Fe-Cr-Si based non-oriented electrical steel sheet having excellent magnetic properties in the high-frequency range, in particular, in a frequency range of 1 kHz or more.

[0010] As a result of intensive research into the problems described above, the present inventors have found that, although the percentage of eddy current loss in the iron loss is generally high in the high-frequency range, the influence of hysteresis loss is relatively large with respect to the Fe-Cr-Si based electrical steel sheet. It has been found that because of a deterioration of hysteresis loss, the decrease in eddy current loss due to high electrical resistance does not sufficiently contribute to high-frequency magnetic properties. It has been discovered that in order to obtain improved hysteresis loss, it is necessary to control the frequency of nitrides containing chromium (nitrides including chromium) in the interior of the steel sheet. The present invention has been achieved based on the findings described above.

Si is a principal element for increasing the electrical resistance of steel. Furthermore, due to the synergic effect with Cr, Si significantly increases electrical resistance, and in particular, Si is an effective element for improving iron loss in the high frequency range. If the Si content is less than about 2.5% by mass, even if Cr is used together, only an electrical resistance that is substantially the same as the electrical resistance of the conventional electrical steel sheet is obtained, and thus it is not possible to obtain satisfactory iron loss in the high frequency range. On the other hand, if the Si content exceeds about 10% by mass, even if Cr is added, toughness that normally allows the steel to be rolled is not ensured. Therefore, the Si content is set at about 2.5% to about 10% by mass. The upper limit may be set at 10.0% by mass.

[0002] In recent years, in terms of prevention of global warming and the like, a further reduction in power consumption in a motor of an air conditioner, a main motor of an electric vehicle, and the like has been required. These motors are often used by being rotated at high speed. Accordingly, a non-oriented electrical steel sheet used for an iron core of a motor has been required to improve (reduce) a core loss in a frequency region of 400 Hz to 800 Hz higher than 50 Hz to 60 Hz being a commercial frequency. This is because the reduction in core loss reduces power consumption, thereby allowing an amount of energy consumption to be reduced.

[0004] On the other hand, since rotating equipment such as motors or generators makes use of electromagnetic phenomenon, the core materials of iron cores of rotating equipment are also required to have excellent magnetic properties. In particular, it is necessary for rotors of high speed motors to assume low iron loss at high frequency; iron loss at high frequency would otherwise lead to a rise in core temperature due to the eddy current induced by a high-frequency magnetic flux, causing thermal demagnetization of embedded permanent magnets, reducing motor efficiency, and so on. Therefore, there is a demand for such an electrical steel sheet as a material for rotors that possesses high strength and excellent magnetic properties.

is 60 µΩcm or more, and the number of nitrides containing chromium per mm 2 in the interior of the steel sheet is 2,500 or less. Consequently, the problem that high electrical resistance resulting from the high Si content and high Cr content is not satisfactorily utilized is advantageously solved, and it is possible to provide a nonoriented electrical steel sheet having excellent magnetic properties in the high-frequency range, in particular; in a frequency range of 1 kHz or more.

detailed_results.question: Why high-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet is needed for high-speed motors like in electric vehicles, and what problems happen with other high-strength materials used before?
detailed_results.ground_truth: High-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet is needed for high-speed motors, such as those used in electric vehicles, because these motors are designed for high-speed rotation to achieve high torque, and the rotor structure includes very narrow bridge portions (1 to 2 mm wide) between magnets and the steel sheet. This narrow width requires the steel sheet to have high strength to prevent failure during operation. Other high-strength materials used before, like non-oriented electrical steel sheets strengthened by adding Mn and Ni, could not provide enough strength and also reduced toughness, leading to poor productivity and yield, and the cost was high due to expensive alloying elements like Ni. Methods using carbonitride dispersion or Cu precipitates also failed to achieve sufficient strength or caused problems like coarsening of crystal grains, reduced fracture elongation, or magnetic aging due to carbide precipitation at motor operating temperatures. Therefore, a high-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet with a specific composition and controlled microstructure is required to meet the demands of high-speed motors in electric vehicles.
contexts: [0001] The present invention relates to a non-oriented electrical steel sheet, and in particular, to a non-oriented electrical steel sheet having high strength and excellent fatigue properties, and furthermore, excellent magnetic properties that is suitably used for components that are subject to high stress, typically, drive motors for turbine generators, electric vehicles and hybrid vehicles, or rotors for high-speed rotating machinery, such as servo motors for robots, machine tools or the like, and a method for manufacturing the same. Additionally, the present invention provides the above-described nonoriented electrical steel sheet at low cost as compared to the conventional art.

[0012] As described above, some proposals have been made on high-strength non-oriented electrical steel sheets. In the proposals made to date, however, it has not been possible until now to manufacture, with the use of an ordinary facility for manufacturing electrical steel sheets, such a high-strength non-oriented electrical steel sheet in an industrially stable manner with good yield and at low cost that has good magnetic properties in addition to high tensile strength and high fatigue strength, and furthermore, satisfy the quality requirements of steel sheet, such as those relating to surface defects, internal defects, sheet shape or the like. Particularly, the high-strength electrical steel sheets that have so far been provided for rotors of high speed motors are in a situation where the resulting rotors will be subject to unavoidable heat generation due to their magnetic property, i.e., high iron loss at high frequency, which necessarily poses limitations on the design specification of the motors.

[0093] As described above, non-oriented electrical steel sheets of the present invention have excellent high-frequency magnetic properties. The steel sheets of the present invention are most suitable for devices used in the high-frequency range, for example, electric car motors, power generators for gas microturbines, and high-frequency reactors, and the industrial values thereof are great.

[0022] According to the present invention, a non-oriented electrical steel sheet may be provided that is excellent in both mechanical properties and magnetic properties required for a rotor material of motors rotating at high speed, and that has excellent quality of steel sheet in terms of scab, sheet shape, and so on. The present invention also allows stable production of such non-oriented electrical steel sheets with high yield, without incurring a significant increase in cost or imposing severe constraints on manufacture or requiring extra steps, as compared to the normal production of non-oriented electrical steel sheets. Therefore, the present invention is applicable in the field of motors, such as drive motors of electric vehicles and hybrid vehicles or servo motors of robots and machine tools, where demand for higher rotational speed is expected to grow in the future. Thus, the present invention has a high industrial value and makes a significant contribution to the industry.

[0002] Recently, devices used in the higher frequency ranges than conventionally used, for example, electric car motors, gas microturbines, and high-frequency reactors, have been increasingly used, and there has been a demand for electrical steel sheets having excellent magnetic properties in the high frequency range. These devices are used in a high frequency range of several hundred hertz to several tens of kilohertz.

[0002] In recent years, higher performance properties are required for a non-oriented electrical steel sheet to be used as an iron core material of a rotary machine due to a worldwide increase in achievement of energy saving of an electrical apparatus. Recently in particular, as a motor to be used for an electric vehicle or the like, a demand for a small-sized high-power motor is high. Such an electric vehicle motor is designed to make high-speed rotation possible to thereby obtain high torque.

[0003] A high-speed rotation motor has also been used for a machine tool and an electrical apparatus such as a vacuum cleaner. The outer shape of a high-speed rotation motor for an electric vehicle is larger than that of a high-speed rotation motor for an electrical apparatus. Further, as a high-speed rotation motor for an electric vehicle, a DC brushless motor has been mainly used. In a DC brushless motor, magnets are embedded in the vicinity of an outer periphery of a rotor. In the above structure, the width of a bridge portion in an outer periphery portion of the rotor (the width between magnets from the most outer periphery of the rotor to a steel sheet) is extremely narrow, which is 1 to 2 mm, depending on a place. Therefore, a high-strength steel sheet has been required for a high-speed rotation motor for an electric vehicle rather than a conventional non-oriented electrical steel sheet.

[0002] In recent years, higher performance properties have been required for a non-oriented electrical steel sheet to be used as an iron core material of a rotary machine due to a worldwide increase in achievement of energy saving of an electrical apparatus. Recently in particular, as a motor to be used for an electric vehicle or the like, a demand for a small-sized high-power motor has been high. Such an electric vehicle motor has been designed to make high-speed rotation possible to thereby obtain high torque.

[0004] On the other hand, since rotating equipment such as motors or generators makes use of electromagnetic phenomenon, the core materials of iron cores of rotating equipment are also required to have excellent magnetic properties. In particular, it is necessary for rotors of high speed motors to assume low iron loss at high frequency; iron loss at high frequency would otherwise lead to a rise in core temperature due to the eddy current induced by a high-frequency magnetic flux, causing thermal demagnetization of embedded permanent magnets, reducing motor efficiency, and so on. Therefore, there is a demand for such an electrical steel sheet as a material for rotors that possesses high strength and excellent magnetic properties.
## (54) HIGH-STRENGTH NON-ORIENTED MAGNETIC STEEL SHEET AND PROCESS FOR PRODUCING THE HIGH-STRENGTH NON-ORIENTED MAGNETIC STEEL SHEET
detailed_results.answer:
High-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheets are essential for high-speed motors in electric vehicles due to several reasons and associated problems with other high-strength materials used previously.

Firstly, as stated in [Source 6] and [Source 8], there is a growing demand for small-sized high-power motors for electric vehicles that can rotate at high speeds to generate high torque. The high-speed rotation necessitates the use of a high-strength steel sheet to withstand the stresses generated during operation.

Secondly, according to [Source 7] and [Source 9], high-speed rotation motors for electric vehicles have a narrow bridge portion in their outer periphery, which requires a high-strength steel sheet to ensure structural integrity. In contrast, conventional non-oriented electrical steel sheets may not meet the strength requirements for these applications.

Lastly, as mentioned in [Source 2], high-speed motors are often subjected to unavoidable heat generation due to their magnetic properties, leading to limitations on motor design specifications. High-strength non-oriented electrical steel sheets with excellent magnetic properties, such as those described in [Source 3] and [Source 4], can help mitigate these issues by reducing iron loss at high frequency, thereby preventing thermal demagnetization of embedded permanent magnets and improving motor efficiency.

However, it is worth noting that, according to [Source 2], the industrial production of high-strength non-oriented electrical steel sheets with good magnetic properties, low cost, and minimal quality compromises has been a challenge until now. The patent excerpts do not provide specific details on the problems encountered with other high-strength materials used before in electric vehicle motors.

detailed_results.question: How high-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet get good high-frequency magnetic properties, and what role controlling nitrides containing chromium play?
detailed_results.ground_truth: High-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet get good high-frequency magnetic properties by controlling the number of nitrides containing chromium inside the steel sheet. If too many nitrides with chromium form during annealing, iron loss gets worse, especially at high frequency. By adjusting the annealing atmosphere, like using less nitrogen gas or adding elements like Al, Sn, or Sb, the amount of nitrides with chromium can be kept low (2,500 or less per mm2), so the steel shows better high-frequency magnetic properties and lower iron loss.

[0010] As a result of intensive research into the problems described above, the present inventors have found that, although the percentage of eddy current loss in the iron loss is generally high in the high-frequency range, the influence of hysteresis loss is relatively large with respect to the Fe-Cr-Si based electrical steel sheet. It has been found that because of a deterioration of hysteresis loss, the decrease in eddy current loss due to high electrical resistance does not sufficiently contribute to high-frequency magnetic properties. It has been discovered that in order to obtain improved hysteresis loss, it is necessary to control the frequency of nitrides containing chromium (nitrides including chromium) in the interior of the steel sheet. The present invention has been achieved based on the findings described above.

detailed_results.answer:
The high-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheets achieve good high-frequency magnetic properties through a specific composition and control of nitrides containing chromium, as detailed in [Source 1], [Source 4], [Source 8], and [Source 10]. According to these sources, the steel contains 2.5% to 10% by mass of Si, 1.5% to 20% by mass of Cr, and various other elements such as Al, Sb, Sn, Ti, Nb, C, N, S, and incidental impurities. The electrical resistivity of the steel is required to be 60 µΩcm or more [Source 1, Source 4].

The number of nitrides containing chromium per mm² in the interior of the steel sheet is controlled to be 2,500 or less [Source 1, Source 4]. This control is crucial because it helps improve hysteresis loss, which is significant in the high-frequency range for Fe-Cr-Si based electrical steel sheets [Source 2]. The decrease in eddy current loss due to high electrical resistance does not sufficiently contribute to high-frequency magnetic properties when hysteresis loss deteriorates [Source 2]. By controlling nitrides containing chromium, the present invention achieves improved hysteresis loss and provides non-oriented electrical steel sheets with excellent magnetic properties in the high-frequency range [Source 1, Source 3].

However, it is not explicitly stated in the provided excerpts how the control of nitrides containing chromium improves hysteresis loss or what specific role these nitrides play in the overall magnetic properties of the steel sheets. Further investigation may be necessary to gain a more comprehensive understanding of this aspect.

detailed_results.question: How does the addition of bismuth (Bi) to a non-oriented electrical steel sheet influence the suppression of Ti inclusions and the resulting magnetic properties, and what compositional and process conditions are necessary to maximize these effects for high-speed motor rotor applications?
detailed_results.ground_truth:
The addition of bismuth (Bi) to a non-oriented electrical steel sheet plays a critical role in suppressing the formation of Ti inclusions, such as TiN, TiS, and TiC, which can otherwise degrade the magnetic properties of the steel. According to the context, when an appropriate amount of Bi is present (typically between 0.001 mass% and 0.01 mass%), the production of Ti inclusions after annealing is reduced, leading to enhanced crystal grain growth and improved magnetic properties (see <9-hop>). This effect is particularly important for high-speed motor rotors, where low core loss and high efficiency are required (<2-hop>, <3-hop>, <4-hop>).

To maximize the beneficial effects of Bi addition, several compositional and process conditions must be met:
- The steel composition should include Si (1.0–3.5 mass%), Al (0.1–3.0 mass%), Mn (0.1–2.0 mass%), Ti (0.001–0.01 mass%), and Bi (0.001–0.01 mass%), with low levels of C (≤0.01 mass%), P (≤0.1 mass%), S (≤0.005 mass%), and N (≤0.005 mass%) (<8-hop>, <11-hop>).
- The Bi must be added to the molten steel shortly before solidification, ideally within three minutes, to ensure sufficient Bi remains in solution and is not lost due to volatilization (<11-hop>).
- The presence of sulfur (S) must be controlled. If S exceeds 0.005 mass%, desulfurizing elements such as rare earth metals (REM) or Ca should be added to reduce free S, preventing Bi from being trapped in MnS and ensuring it remains available to suppress Ti inclusions (<10-hop>).

When these conditions are satisfied, Bi effectively suppresses the precipitation of TiN and TiS during annealing and TiC during stress relief annealing, resulting in fewer inclusions, larger grain size, and improved magnetic properties, such as lower core loss (W10/800 or W10/1000) (<9-hop>, <11-hop>). This directly supports the requirements for high-speed motor rotors, where both high tensile strength and low high-frequency core loss are essential for efficiency and miniaturization (<2-hop>, <3-hop>, <4-hop>).

Additionally, the present invention provides the above-described nonoriented electrical steel sheet at low cost as compared to the conventional art.

[0026] Further, it is also found out that as the Ti content of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet becomes higher, the Bi content necessary for obtaining the good magnetic property also becomes higher. However, when the Bi content exceeds 0.01 mass%, a large number of inclusions containing Bi exist, and thereby the magnetic property becomes poor. Consequently, the Bi content of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet is required to be 0.01 mass% or less.

[0003] A high-speed rotation motor has also been used for a machine tool and an electrical apparatus such as a vacuum cleaner. The outer shape of a high-speed rotation motor for an electric vehicle is larger than that of a high-speed rotation motor for an electrical apparatus. Further, as a high-speed rotation motor for an electric vehicle, a DC brushless motor has been mainly used. In a DC brushless motor, magnets are embedded in the vicinity of an outer periphery of a rotor. In the above structure, the width of a bridge portion in an outer periphery portion of the rotor (the width between magnets from the most outer periphery of the rotor to a steel sheet) is extremely narrow, which is 1 to 2 mm, depending on a place. Therefore, a high-strength steel sheet has been required for a high-speed rotation motor for an electric vehicle rather than a conventional non-oriented electrical steel sheet.

Thus, as shown in Fig. 1, (1) expression, and (2) expression, it is conceivable that as the Ti content becomes larger, the Bi content necessary for reducing Ti inclusions is increased, and a proportional relationship is established between the Ti content and the Bi content.

[0002] In recent years, higher performance properties are required for a non-oriented electrical steel sheet to be used as an iron core material of a rotary machine due to a worldwide increase in achievement of energy saving of an electrical apparatus. Recently in particular, as a motor to be used for an electric vehicle or the like, a demand for a small-sized high-power motor is high. Such an electric vehicle motor is designed to make high-speed rotation possible to thereby obtain high torque.
[0001] The present invention relates to a non-oriented electrical steel sheet suitable for a rotor of a high-speed rotary machine.

[0002] A non-oriented electrical steel sheet is used for a rotor of a rotary machine or the like, for example. In general, centrifugal force acting on the rotor is in proportion to a rotation radius, and is in proportion to the square of a rotation speed. For the above reason, extremely large stress acts on the rotor of the high-speed rotary machine. Thus, tensile strength of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet for a rotor is preferably high. That is, the non-oriented electrical steel sheet for a rotor preferably has high tension. As described above, high tensile strength (high tension) is required for the non-oriented electrical steel sheet for a rotor.

Particularly, as a larger amount of MnS exists in the non-oriented electrical steel sheet, the amount of Bi to be compositely precipitated in MnS is also increased, so that Ti inclusions are not likely to be reduced.
detailed_results.answer:
The addition of bismuth (Bi) to a non-oriented electrical steel sheet significantly influences the suppression of Ti inclusions and the resulting magnetic properties. According to [Source 2], [Source 3], and [Source 6], as the Bi content increases, Ti inclusions are reduced, and crystal grains are likely to grow, thereby improving the magnetic property. However, when the Bi content exceeds 0.01 mass%, the magnetic property becomes poor due to a large number of Bi inclusions ([Source 2]).

The optimal range for Bi content in the steel sheet is not explicitly stated in the provided excerpts, but it is suggested that an appropriate amount of Bi should be contained in the non-oriented electrical steel sheet to reduce Ti inclusions and improve magnetic properties ([Source 3], [Source 6]). The exact amount might depend on the Ti content, as shown in [Source 6] where a proportional relationship between the Ti content and the Bi content is established.

For high-speed motor rotor applications, a high-strength steel sheet is required due to the large stress acting on the rotor ([Source 5]). The patent excerpts suggest that the addition of bismuth can help meet this requirement by reducing Ti inclusions and improving the magnetic properties of the steel sheet ([Source 3], [Source 6]).

It's worth noting that the mechanism in which the production of Ti inclusions is suppressed when Bi is contained in the non-oriented electrical steel sheet has not been fully clarified yet ([Source 6]), and more studies might be needed to optimize the compositional and process conditions for maximizing these effects.

The patent excerpts do not provide specific technical details or formulas regarding the manufacturing process of the steel sheets with added bismuth. Further investigation is required to understand the exact process conditions necessary to achieve the desired properties for high-speed motor rotor applications.

detailed_results.question: How does forming an Fe-Ni alloy film on a high-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet affect its magnetic properties, and what compositional considerations are important for both the steel sheet and the Fe-Ni alloy film?
detailed_results.ground_truth: Forming an Fe-Ni alloy film on a high-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet significantly improves the relative permeability and reduces the core loss of the material. The optimal improvement is achieved when the Fe-Ni alloy film contains 60% to 90% Ni and has a thickness of at least 0.1 μm, with the effect saturating above 0.6 μm. For the steel sheet itself, key compositional considerations include maintaining C content at 0.05% or less to avoid increased core loss, Si content between 0.1% and 7% for sufficient resistivity without compromising cold-rolling performance, and Al content between 0.01% and 7% for electrical resistance and deoxidation. Additionally, Mn, Ti, N, S, Sn, Cu, and Ni should be controlled within specified ranges to optimize magnetic properties and inhibit surface nitridation and oxidation during annealing.
contexts: [0027] The present inventors learned from these results of experiments that by forming the Fe-Ni alloy film on a surface of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet, the relative permeability and the core loss are dramatically improved. Further, the present inventors also learned that it is necessary that the Ni content of the Fe-Ni alloy film is 60% to 90% aid the thickness of the Fe-Ni alloy film is 0.1 Pm or more for improving the relative permeability and the core loss. That is, one surface of a non-oriented electrical steel sheet was coated with Fe-Ni alloy films having different thicknesses. As the non-oriented electrical steel sheet, one containing C: 0.003%, Si: 2.4%, and Al: 0.5%, a balance of which being composed of Fe and inevitable impurities, and having a thickness of 0.35 mm was used. Further, the Ni contents of the Fe-Ni alloy films were set to 78% in any of the thicknesses. Then, a DC magnetizing property was measured. When measuring the DC magnetizing property, the maximum magnetic flux density Bm was set to 0.8 T, and a relationship between the thickness (a coating thickness) of the Fe-Ni alloy film and the relative permeability Ps was obtained. Further, similar measurement was also conducted in one having the Fe-Ni alloy films formed on both surfaces of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet. Results thereof are shown in Fig. 4.

[0039] The Fe-Ni alloy film is preferably composed of Fe: 10% to 40% and Ni: 90% to 60%, and more preferably composed of Fe: 15% to 30% and Ni: 85% to 70%. This is to obtain a good magnetic property in a low magnetic field. Incidentally, other metallic elements such as Mo may also be contained in the Fe-Ni alloy film. In the above case, the Fe-Ni alloy film preferably contains Fe: 10% to 40% and Ni: 90 to 60%, and more preferably contains Fe: 15% to 30% and Ni: 85% to 70%.

[0024] The reason why the relative permeability Ps is extremely low when the thickness of the Fe-Ni alloy film is less than 0.1 Pm is that magnetic domains on a surface of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet are not easily aligned despite forming the Fe-Ni alloy film. Further, the reason why the effect of improving the relative permeability Ps is saturated is that as long as the magnetic domains on the surface of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet are sufficiently aligned, even though the Fe-Ni alloy film having the thickness of the above or more is formed, the magnetic domains are not easily aligned any more.

[0026] This is because when excitation, a magnetic flux concentrates in a region to be most easily magnetized in a thickness direction of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet. That is, the reason is that although the Fe-Ni alloy film is formed on a single region, (one surface) in the thickness direction, or the Fe-Ni alloy films are formed on two regions (both surfaces) in the thickness direction, it is in a single region that the magnetic flux concentrates most.
detailed_results.answer:
Forming an Fe-Ni alloy film on a high-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet significantly improves its magnetic properties, particularly the relative permeability and core loss [Source 1]. The key compositional considerations for the Fe-Ni alloy film are a Ni content of 60% to 90% and a thickness of 0.1 Pm or more [Source 1]. For the steel sheet, it is important that it contains specific amounts of C, Si, and Al, with typical values being C: 0.003%, Si: 2.4%, and Al: 0.5% [Source 2]. The thickness of the steel sheet can vary, but a common value is 0.35 mm [Source 2, Source 4].

The Fe-Ni alloy films can be formed using an electroplating or immersion plating method [Source 1, Source 4, Source 6]. The thickness of the Fe-Ni alloy film can range from 0.05 Pm to 0.8 Pm, with optimal values for improving magnetic properties being around 0.5 Pm per one surface [Source 4]. The Ni content of the Fe-Ni alloy films can vary from 0% to 100%, but optimal values are between 78% and 60% when the thickness is constant at 0.3 Pm [Source 5].

The reason for these specific compositional requirements is that they allow for the alignment of magnetic domains on the surface of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet, which improves its magnetic properties [Source 9]. However, it's important to note that when the thickness of the Fe-Ni alloy film is less than 0.1 Pm, the relative permeability remains extremely low due to the difficulty in aligning magnetic domains [Source 9]. Additionally, once the magnetic domains are sufficiently aligned, further increases in the thickness of the Fe-Ni alloy film do not significantly improve the magnetic properties any further [Source 9].

It's also worth mentioning that the Fe-Ni alloy films can contain other metallic elements such as Mo to further enhance their magnetic properties [Source 8]. However, the patent does not provide specific details on the effects of these additional elements.

In summary, forming an Fe-Ni alloy film on a high-strength non-oriented magnetic steel sheet improves its magnetic properties by allowing for the alignment of magnetic domains. The key compositional considerations are a Ni content of 60% to 90% and a thickness of 0.1 Pm or more for the Fe-Ni alloy film, and specific amounts of C, Si, and Al for the steel sheet. Further research may be needed to understand the effects of additional metallic elements in the Fe-Ni alloy films. [Source 1, Source 2, Source 4, Source 5, Source 8, Source 9]

detailed_results.question: What steel slab components in Table 3 give better magnetic properties, and how does rolling reduction affect results?
detailed_results.ground_truth: Steel slabs in Table 3 with Sn (condition 13) or Cu (condition 14) have better magnetic properties than those without Sn and Cu (condition 11). Also, rolling reduction in the second cold-rolling affects results: when it is less than 40% (condition 12) or over 85% (condition 15), magnetic properties are worse than when it is around 68% (conditions 11, 13, 14).
contexts: [0041] First, steel slabs each containing components presented in Table 3 and a balance composed of Fe and inevitable impurities were produced. Then, hot-rolling of each steel slab was conducted to produce a steel strip (hot-rolled sheet), and cold-rolling was performed twice. At this time, the first cold-rolling was started without performing hot-rolled sheet annealing after the hot-rolling, and intermediate annealing was conducted at 950°C for 2 minutes between the two times of the cold-rolling. A thickness of each steel strip after the cold-rolling was set to 0.35 mm. Finish temperatures in the hot-rolling, thicknesses of the hot-rolled sheets, thicknesses of the steel strips after performing the first cold-rolling, and rolling reductions in the second cold-rolling are presented in Table 4. After performing the second cold-rolling, finish annealing was performed at 970°C for 40 seconds. As is apparent from table 4, a rolling reduction in the first cold-rolling was set to approximately 40%. Further, a sample was taken from each steel strip after the finish annealing, and as magnetic properties thereof, a magnetic flux density B50 and an iron loss W10/400 were measured. The iron loss W10/400 is an iron loss under a condition where a frequency is 400 Hz, and the maximum magnetic flux density is 1.0T. Results of these are also presented in Table 4.

[0032] It can be understood that in the condition where the hot-rolled sheet annealing is not performed, the magnetic properties in the rolling direction of the non-oriented electrical steel sheet can be significantly improved by appropriately combining the finish temperature in the hot-rolling and the rolling reduction in the second cold-rolling, as seen from Table 2. In other words, it can be said that when the finish temperature in the hot-rolling is 900°C or less, and the rolling reduction in the second cold-rolling is not less than 40% nor more than 85%, it is possible to obtain extremely good magnetic properties in the rolling direction.

[0010] The present inventors repeatedly conducted earnest studies from a point of view in which magnetic properties in a rolling direction in a non-oriented electrical steel sheet are improved by changing conditions of contents of respective components, treatment before cold-rolling, the number of times of the cold-rolling, a rolling reduction in the cold-rolling and the like.

[0045] Further, in the condition No. 13, in which Sn was contained, and the condition No. 14, in which Cu was contained, the magnetic properties in the rolling direction were better than those in the condition No. 11, in which Sn and Cu were not contained. As seen from the results, it can be understood that when Sn or Cu is contained, the magnetic properties in the rolling direction are further improved. Moreover, as is apparent from Table 4, it can be understood that, according to the examples of the present invention, it is possible to manufacture the non-oriented electrical steel sheets excellent in magnetic properties in the rolling direction.

[0029] The present inventors first produced steel slabs each containing components presented in Table 1 and a balance composed of Fe and inevitable impurities. Then, hot-rolling of each steel slab was conducted so as to produce a steel strip (hot-rolled sheet), and cold-rolling was performed twice. At this time, the first cold-rolling was started without performing hot-rolled sheet annealing after the hot-rolling, and intermediate annealing was conducted at 1000°C for 1 minute between the two times of cold-rolling. A thickness of each steel strip after the cold-rolling (cold-rolled sheet) was set to 0.35 mm. Finish temperatures in the hot-rolling, thicknesses of the hot-rolled sheets, thicknesses of the steel strips after the first cold-rolling, and rolling reductions in the second cold-rolling are presented in Table 2. After performing the second cold-rolling, finish annealing was performed at 950°C for 30 seconds. As is apparent from table 2, a rolling reduction in the first cold-rolling was set to 31.4% to 36.4%. Then, a sample was taken from each steel strip after the finish annealing, and as magnetic properties thereof, a magnetic flux density B50 and an iron loss W15/50 were measured. Here, the iron loss W15/50 is an iron loss under a condition where a frequency is 50 Hz, and the maximum magnetic flux density is 1.5T. Results of these are also presented in Table 2.

[0050] Further, in the conditions No. 23 and No. 24, in which Cr was contained, the iron loss in the rolling direction was significantly low, compared to that in the condition No. 21, in which Cr was not contained. As seen from the results, it can be understood that when Cr is contained, the iron loss in the rolling direction is further suppressed. Moreover, as is apparent from Table 6, it can be understood that, according to the examples of the present invention, it is possible to manufacture the non-oriented electrical steel sheets excellent in magnetic properties in the rolling direction.

Further, the present inventors came to the following manufacturing method of a non-oriented electrical steel sheet.

[0027] The present inventors confirmed these effects by the following experiments. First, a slab containing C: 0.017 mass%, Si: 3.12 mass%, Al: 0.65 mass%, Ni: 2.54 mass%, P: 0.02 mass%, N: 0.003 mass%, and Ti: 0.18 mass% was hot-rolled, and a hot-rolled steel sheet was obtained. The value R Ti of the above hot-rolled steel sheet is 2.3. Next, the hot-rolled steel sheet was cold-rolled to have four types of thicknesses shown in Table 1, and a cold-rolled steel sheet was obtained. Thereafter, continuous finish annealing at 780°C for 20 seconds was applied to the cold-rolled steel sheet, and a non-oriented electrical steel sheet was obtained. Then, Epstein samples and tensile test pieces were cut out from the non-oriented electrical steel sheet, and magnetic properties and mechanical properties were measured by using them. Results thereof are also shown in Table 1. Further, "YP" represents a yield point, "TS" represents the tensile strength, and "EL" represents elongation.

Note that as an aim to avoid embrittlement of the crystal grain boundaries with the increase in tension, B may also be contained. In the above case, a B content is preferably 0.001 mass% or more. On the other hand, when the B content exceeds 0.007 mass%, a reduction in a magnetic flux density, embrittlement at the time of hot rolling, and the like are caused. Thus, the B content is preferably 0.007 mass% or less.

Ti and Nb are considered as workability-improving components in ordinary Cr-containing steel. However, Ti and Nb deteriorate magnetic properties. In the present invention, since workability is improved by the addition of Cr and reductions in the contents of C and N, workability improvement capabilities of Ti and Nb are not required. Therefore, in view of magnetic properties, the contents of Ti and Nb are desirably reduced as much as possible.
